title: Magnetism: From First Principles
description: Understanding magnetism from moving charges to real magnets.

sections.title: What Causes Magnetism?
sections.content: Magnetism arises from moving electric charges. When charges move, they create a magnetic field that influences other moving charges. This means magnetism is not a separate force in isolation, but a consequence of electric fields and motion.

sections.title: A Straight Wire (Current)
sections.content: When current flows through a wire, charges move. This produces a circular magnetic field around the wire. The direction depends on the current direction, and the strength increases with current.

sections.title: A Solenoid (Coiled Wire)
sections.content: A solenoid is many loops of wire stacked together. Each loop produces a magnetic field, and together they combine to form a strong, nearly uniform field inside the coil.

sections.title: A Bar Magnet
sections.content: A bar magnet is made of many tiny atomic current loops. When these align, they produce a large-scale magnetic field. This means a magnet is effectively a collection of aligned microscopic currents.
